title: Tailoring semisynthetic ligands to investigate and regulate ErbB receptor family dysregulation in cancer
author: Carsten Kroll
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: ErbB/HER receptors are receptor tyrosine kinases and play crucial roles in cellular processes. While critical for normal signaling processes, the overexpression and mutation of ErbB receptors is associated with the malignancy of many cancers. Activation of ErbB receptors (EGFR, ErbB2/3/4) by ligand binding induces receptor homo- or heterodimerization that activates the inherent kinases, which then leads to signaling through the PI3K/AKT or the MAP kinase pathways. Accordingly, these receptors and pathways have emerged as targets for cancer therapy. Some tumors however show resistance, for example through upregulation of ErbB3. Recently we focused on biasing ErbB3 receptor interactions with semisynthetic bivalent neuregulin ligands. Neuregulin is the endogenous ligand for ErbB3 and the bivalent ligands are supposed to bias ErbB3 signaling by trapping ErbB3 into inactive homodimers, since ErbB3 can only signal through heterodimers. We developed a robust semisynthetic strategy that allows us access to a variety of bivalent cytokines. It relies on recombinant expression of Neuregulin, modified with short amino acid sequences for sortase mediated ligation. The ligation modifies Neuregulin with chemically-synthesized peptidic components that include either a norbornene or tetrazine moiety, which undergo a metal-free bioorthogonal ligation to link the two neuregulins. Due to the synthetic linker, modifications and tags can be easily incorporated.  This powerful approach provides a tailored set of bivalent ligands for studies on the ideal construct for sequestering ErbB3 and mitigating the deleterious effects of receptor overepression. Additionally, the semisynthetic strategy provides valuable reagents for visualizing the dynamic interactions amongst the ErbB receptors and other cell surface proteins.
